## Title: Fix integer truncation in file descriptor getter functions

### Summary
File descriptors can exceed the range of a 32-bit `int` on some systems or under certain runtime conditions. The `device_get_fd()` and `socket_get_fd()` functions were returning `int`, which could cause integer truncation when the actual file descriptor value is stored or passed through function pointers. This change widens the return type to `long int` to safely accommodate file descriptor values across all supported platforms.

### Changes
- `openair3/NAS/COMMON/UTIL/device.c`: Changed `device_get_fd()` return type from `int` to `long int`
- `openair3/NAS/COMMON/UTIL/device.h`: Updated function declaration for `device_get_fd()`
- `openair3/NAS/COMMON/UTIL/socket.c`: Changed `socket_get_fd()` return type from `int` to `long int`
- `openair3/NAS/COMMON/UTIL/socket.h`: Updated function declaration for `socket_get_fd()`
- `openair3/NAS/UE/API/USER/user_api_defs.h`: Updated `getfd` function pointer typedef from `int (*getfd)(const void*)` to `long int (*getfd)(const void*)`

### Implementation Details
The change maintains backward compatibility while preventing potential integer overflow. The `fd` field within the internal `device_id_s` and `socket_id_s` structures remains typed as `int` (matching the system type for file descriptors), but the getter functions now promote the value to `long int` for safe propagation through the NAS layer's generic function pointer interface. This ensures consistent type handling across all call sites without requiring changes to the underlying socket or device implementations.

    49	/****************************************************************************/
    50	/****************  E X T E R N A L    D E F I N I T I O N S  ****************/
    51	/****************************************************************************/
    52	
    53	/****************************************************************************/
    54	/*******************  L O C A L    D E F I N I T I O N S  *******************/
    55	/****************************************************************************/
    56	
    57	/* -------------------------------------
    58	 * Identifier of network socket endpoint
    59	 * -------------------------------------
    60	 *  A network socket endpoint is defined with a type (client or server),
    61	 *  a port number, and the name and address of the remote host it should
    62	 *  be connected to. A socket file descriptor is created to handle the
    63	 *  setup of the communication channel with the remote peer.
    64	 */
    65	struct socket_id_s {
    66	  int type; /* connection type (client/server)  */
    67	  int port; /* port number      */
    68	#define SOCKET_HOSTNAME_SIZE  32
    69	  char rhost[SOCKET_HOSTNAME_SIZE]; /* remote hostname    */
    70	  struct sockaddr_storage addr; /* remote address   */
    71	  int fd; /* socket file descriptor */
    72	};
    73	
    74	/* Set socket option at the sockets API level (SOL_SOCKET) */
    75	static int _socket_set_option(int sfd);
    76	
    77	/****************************************************************************/
    78	/******************  E X P O R T E D    F U N C T I O N S  ******************/
    79	/****************************************************************************/
    80	
    81	/****************************************************************************
    82	 **                                                                        **
    83	 ** Name:  socket_udp_open()                              
... [truncated] ...
***/
   106	void* socket_udp_open(int type, const char* host, const char* port)
   107	{
   108	  struct addrinfo socket_info; /* endpoint information    */
   109	  struct addrinfo *socket_addr, *sp; /* endpoint address    */
   110	  int sfd=-1; /* socket file descriptor  */
   111	
   112	  /*
   113	   * Parameters sanity check
   114	   * -----------------------
   115	   * The local connection endpoint shall be of type CLIENT or SERVER
   116	   */
   117	  if (host == NULL) {
   118	    type = SOCKET_SERVER;
   119	  } else if (type != SOCKET_CLIENT && type != SOCKET_SERVER) {
   120	    return NULL;
   121	  }
   122	
   123	  /*
   124	   * Initialize the endpoint address information
   125	   * -------------------------------------------
   126	   *  The AI_PASSIVE flag allows "wildcard address" when hostname is not
   127	   *  provided (NULL). The wildcard address is used by applications (ty-
   128	   *  pically servers) that intend to accept connections on any of the
   129	   *  hosts's network addresses. If the hostname is not NULL, then the
   130	   *  AI_PASSIVE flag is ignored.
   131	   *  When the AI_V4MAPPED flag is set, and AF_INET6 is specified, and no
   132	   *  matching IPv6 addresses could be found, then IPv4-mapped IPv6 addresses
   133	   *  are returned by getaddrinfo in the list pointed to by result.
   134	   */
   135	  memset (&socket_info, 0, sizeof(struct addrinfo));
   136	  socket_info.ai_socktype = SOCK_DGRAM; /* Datagram socket   */
   137	  socket_info.ai_flags = AI_NUMERICSERV; /* numeric port number  */
   138	
   139	  if (type != SOCKET_CLIENT) {
   140	    /* Setup socket address options at the server side */
   141	    socket_info.ai_family = AF_INET6; /* Accept either IPv4 or IPv6
   142	         * connections     */
   143	    socket_info.ai_flags |= AI_PASSIVE; /* Use "wildcard address"  */
   144	    socket_info.ai_flags |= AI_V4MAPPED; /* IPv4-mapped IPv6 address */
   145	  } else {
   146	    /* Setup socket address options at the client side */
   147	    socket_info.ai_family = AF_INET; /* Any address family   */
   148	    //         socket_info.ai_flags |= AI_V4MAPPED; /* IPv4-mapped IPv6 address */
   149	  }
   150	
   151	  /*
   152	   * getaddrinfo() returns a linked list of address structures:
   153	   *  - The network host may be multi-homed, accessible over multiple
   154	   *    protocols (e.g. both AF_INET and AF_INET6);
   155	   *  - The same service (port number) may be available from multiple
   156	   *    socket types (one SOCK_STREAM address and another SOCK_DGRAM address);
   157	   */
   158	  int rc = getaddrinfo (host, port, &socket_info, &socket_addr);
   159	
   160	  if (rc != 0) {
   161	    if (rc != EAI_SYSTEM) {
   162	      errno = rc;
   163	    }
   164	
   165	    return NULL;
   166	  }
   167	
   168	  /*
   169	   * Try each address until we successfully connect
   170	   */
   171	  for (sp = socket_addr; sp != NULL; sp = sp->ai_next) {
   172	    /* Create the socket endpoint for communication */
   173	    sfd = socket (sp->ai_family, sp->ai_socktype, sp->ai_protocol);
   174	
   175	    if (sfd < 0) {
   176	      continue;
   177	    }
   178	
   179	    /*
   180	     * Initiate a communication channel at the CLIENT side
   181	     */
   182	    if (type == SOCKET_CLIENT) {
   183	      /* Connect the socket to the remote server's address */
   184	      if (connect (sfd, sp->ai_addr, sp->ai_addrlen) != -1) {
   185	        break; /* Connection succeed */
   186	      }
   187	    }
   188	
   189	    /*
   190	     * Initiate a communication channel at the SERVER side
   191	     */
   192	    else {
